title: The Value of Domain Knowledge in Software
excerpt: Something that I think is often overlooked by many software developers is the value of domain knowledge, i.e. understanding the industry you're in and the mindset of the end-users of your software I actually think that explicitly spending time to acquire domain knowledge might be one of the best uses of your time as a software developer.
content_html:
Tinkerer

Code and Climate Change. Blog about software development in ClimateTech

Something that I think is often overlooked by many software developers is the value of domain knowledge, i.e. understanding the industry you’re in and the mindset of the end-users of your software1.

I actually think that explicitly spending time to acquire domain knowledge might be one of the best uses of your time as a software developer for a few reasons:

  • It allows you to act with significantly more autonomy. If you lack domain knowledge, it’s hard to evaluate whether user-facing decisions are a good idea, so you’ll have to get feedback from someone who does know.
    If you live in a fairytale where all tasks are perfectly specified up front with no room for ambiguity this might not be a problem you have. For the rest of us, having domain knowledge allows skipping a lot of back and forth because you often already have the knowledge you need to make decisions.

  • Domain knowledge is great when scoping new projects or features. Having both domain knowledge so you know what to build and the technical experience to understand how to fit it into the existing product is a good skillset to have when figuring out what an MVP looks like.

  • It’s just plain fun. Getting to learn about a new industry and domain is one of the really cool things about building software in my opinion.

Types of Knowledge

In my mind, you can split the knowledge required to be effective at building software into four (extremely coarse) categories:

Foundational Knowledge

The foundational knowledge about how computers and their building blocks work. This could be about how processors work, about complexities of algorithms, client/server infrastructure, when and how to cache, etc.

This kind of knowledge is often something that’s picked up via formal education, but can also be picked up via reading books, or sometimes by learning painful lessons on the job.
This kind of knowledge generally carries over across jobs, and is useful in almost all situations.

Stack-specific Knowledge

Then there’s knowledge that’s specific to your stack. How to make the best use of your programming language, what weird quirks or footguns it has2 or how to best make use of the framework on top of your programming language.
This knowledge generally carries over across jobs if you’re using the same stack, but if you’re starting over on a completely new stack you’ll have to re-learn new stack-specific knowledge.

Product Knowledge

Next up is the knowledge about the product or service you’re working on right this moment. This is generally always going to be job-specific and it means that when you switch jobs you’ll always have to re-learn this.
Depending on how many products your company has, you might even have to learn this multiple times in the same job.

Domain Knowledge

Last is the knowledge about the domain your end-users are operating in. Personally I think domain knowledge is so broad that it’s hard to define, but here’s some examples:

  • When working with wind turbine software it turned out to be really handy to know a lot about how wind turbines worked even, if I never had to interact with one physically.
    I read the Wind Energy Handbook and actually ended up teaching a “Wind Turbine for Software Developers” course to other companies in the industry.
  • When working with carbon emissions estimations at Climatiq reading the relevant standards, like the Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Standard about how carbon accounting works, helped me make lots of decisions much quicker than if I had needed someone to explain the relevant context to me each time.
  • When I was doing a startup in the waste management industry we interviewed waste haulers, and actually also got to do a full work-day with one of them, to see what their day-to-day was like.

As you can see, domain knowledge can take many forms. The three example above cover physical/mechanical constraints, industry standards, and end-user behaviour / mindset.

In the end we’re trying to get enough big-picture knowledge, that we can easily put ourselves in our users shoes and therefore make good decisions that takes them into account.

How to learn domain knowledge

I think that we acquire the different types of knowledge differently.

I think it’s fair to say that most of the product knowledge and stack knowledge comes from the day-to-day work.
When starting on a new stack or product, you might do some tutorials and read some guides, but afterward the learning comes naturally as part of the regular workflow.

On the opposite end of the spectrum, I think that both foundational knowledge and domain knowledge requires explicit “learning time”.

This is because they often require stepping back from the task that’s right ahead of us to look at the bigger picture.

While foundational knowledge is often something that you’ve been taught as part of an education, domain knowledge isn’t, as it’s well.. domain-specific.

Some ways to acquire domain knowledge is:

  • Reading introductory textbooks in your domain
  • Attending introductory courses in your domain
  • Talking to end-users that are using your product.

I think many of us might feel we’re too busy to take a step back and schedule time for getting the domain knowledge we need.
But we should take that time. It pays off in the long run.

  1. Depending on how large systems you’re working with, you might have users of your software module, and those might have end-users. The distinction can get fairly blurry in large-enough systems, but let’s consider understanding the final end-users along with any direct users, as part of relevant domain knowledge. ↩

  2. Python mutable default arguments, I’m looking at you. ↩

Did you enjoy this post? Please share it!

• It allows you to act with significantly more autonomy. If you lack domain knowledge, it’s hard to evaluate whether user-facing decisions are a good idea, so you’ll have to get feedback from someone who does know.

sentences.en: This could be about how processors work, about complexities of algorithms, client/server infrastructure, when and how to cache, etc.
sentences.zh: 这可能与处理器如何工作、算法的复杂性、客户端/服务器基础设施、何时以及如何缓存等有关。

blocks.html_en:
This kind of knowledge is often something that’s picked up via formal education, but can also be picked up via reading books, or sometimes by learning painful lessons on the job.
This kind of knowledge generally carries over across jobs, and is useful in almost all situations.

sentences.en: This kind of knowledge generally carries over across jobs, and is useful in almost all situations.
sentences.zh: 这种知识通常适用于各种工作，并且在几乎所有情况下都很有用。

sentences.en: Stack-specific Knowledge

This knowledge generally carries over across jobs if you’re using the same stack, but if you’re starting over on a completely new stack you’ll have to re-learn new stack-specific knowledge.

sentences.en: Then there’s knowledge that’s specific to your stack.

sentences.en: How to make the best use of your programming language, what weird quirks or footguns it has2 or how to best make use of the framework on top of your programming language.

sentences.en: This knowledge generally carries over across jobs if you’re using the same stack, but if you’re starting over on a completely new stack you’ll have to re-learn new stack-specific knowledge.

sentences.zh: 产品知识

blocks.html_en:
Next up is the knowledge about the product or service you’re working on right this moment. This is generally always going to be job-specific and it means that when you switch jobs you’ll always have to re-learn this.
Depending on how many products your company has, you might even have to learn this multiple times in the same job.

sentences.en: This is generally always going to be job-specific and it means that when you switch jobs you’ll always have to re-learn this.
sentences.zh: 这通常总是针对具体工作的，这意味着当你换工作时，你总是必须重新学习这一点。

sentences.en: Depending on how many products your company has, you might even have to learn this multiple times in the same job.
sentences.zh: 根据您公司拥有的产品数量，您甚至可能需要在同一份工作中多次学习这一点。

sentences.zh: 领域知识

• When working with wind turbine software it turned out to be really handy to know a lot about how wind turbines worked even, if I never had to interact with one physically.

sentences.en: When working with wind turbine software it turned out to be really handy to know a lot about how wind turbines worked even, if I never had to interact with one physically.
sentences.zh: 当使用风力涡轮机软件时，即使我不需要与风力涡轮机进行物理交互，了解很多关于风力涡轮机如何工作的信息也非常方便。

sentences.en: I read the Wind Energy Handbook and actually ended up teaching a “Wind Turbine for Software Developers” course to other companies in the industry.
sentences.zh: 我阅读了《风能手册》，实际上最终向业内其他公司教授了“软件开发人员风力涡轮机”课程。

blocks.html_en: • When working with carbon emissions estimations at Climatiq reading the relevant standards, like the Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Standard about how carbon accounting works, helped me make lots of decisions much quicker than if I had needed someone to explain the relevant context to me each time.

sentences.en: When working with carbon emissions estimations at Climatiq reading the relevant standards, like the Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Standard about how carbon accounting works, helped me make lots of decisions much quicker than if I had needed someone to explain the relevant context to me each time.
sentences.zh: 在 Climatiq 进行碳排放估算时，阅读相关标准（例如关于碳核算如何运作的《温室气体协议企业标准》），帮助我比每次都需要有人向我解释相关背景更快地做出许多决策。

blocks.html_en: • When I was doing a startup in the waste management industry we interviewed waste haulers, and actually also got to do a full work-day with one of them, to see what their day-to-day was like.

sentences.en: When I was doing a startup in the waste management industry we interviewed waste haulers, and actually also got to do a full work-day with one of them, to see what their day-to-day was like.
sentences.zh: 当我在废物管理行业创业时，我们采访了废物运输商，实际上还与其中一个人一起工作了一整天，看看他们的日常生活是什么样的。

blocks.html_en: As you can see, domain knowledge can take many forms. The three example above cover physical/mechanical constraints, industry standards, and end-user behaviour / mindset.

sentences.en: As you can see, domain knowledge can take many forms.
sentences.zh: 正如您所看到的，领域知识可以采取多种形式。

sentences.en: The three example above cover physical/mechanical constraints, industry standards, and end-user behaviour / mindset.
sentences.zh: 上面的三个例子涵盖了物理/机械约束、行业标准和最终用户行为/心态。

blocks.html_en: In the end we’re trying to get enough big-picture knowledge, that we can easily put ourselves in our users shoes and therefore make good decisions that takes them into account.

sentences.en: In the end we’re trying to get enough big-picture knowledge, that we can easily put ourselves in our users shoes and therefore make good decisions that takes them into account.
sentences.zh: 最后，我们试图获得足够的全局知识，以便我们可以轻松地设身处地为用户着想，从而做出考虑到他们的正确决策。

sentences.en: domain-specific.
sentences.zh: 特定领域的。

blocks.html_en: Some ways to acquire domain knowledge is:

sentences.en: Some ways to acquire domain knowledge is:
sentences.zh: 获取领域知识的一些方法是：

blocks.html_en: • Reading introductory textbooks in your domain

sentences.en: Reading introductory textbooks in your domain
sentences.zh: 阅读您所在领域的入门教科书

blocks.html_en: • Attending introductory courses in your domain

sentences.en: Attending introductory courses in your domain
sentences.zh: 参加您所在领域的入门课程

blocks.html_en: • Talking to end-users that are using your product.

sentences.en: Talking to end-users that are using your product.
sentences.zh: 与使用您产品的最终用户交谈。

blocks.html_en:
I think many of us might feel we’re too busy to take a step back and schedule time for getting the domain knowledge we need.
But we should take that time. It pays off in the long run.

sentences.en: I think many of us might feel we’re too busy to take a step back and schedule time for getting the domain knowledge we need.
sentences.zh: 我想我们中的许多人可能会觉得我们太忙了，无法退后一步并安排时间来获取我们所需的领域知识。

sentences.en: But we should take that time.
sentences.zh: 但我们应该花点时间。

sentences.en: It pays off in the long run.
sentences.zh: 从长远来看，这是有回报的。

blocks.html_en: • Depending on how large systems you’re working with, you might have users of your software module, and those might have end-users. The distinction can get fairly blurry in large-enough systems, but let’s consider understanding the final end-users along with any direct users, as part of relevant domain knowledge. ↩

content_html_zh:
修补匠

守则与气候变化。 有关 ClimateTech 软件开发的博客

我认为许多软件开发人员经常忽视的是领域知识的价值，即了解您所在的行业以及软件最终用户的心态1。

实际上，我认为作为软件开发人员，明确地花时间获取领域知识可能是最好的利用时间之一，原因如下：

  • 它使您可以更加自主地采取行动。 如果您缺乏领域知识，则很难评估面向用户的决策是否是一个好主意，因此您必须从了解的人那里获得反馈。 如果您生活在一个童话故事中，所有任务都预先完美指定，没有任何歧义的余地，这可能不是您遇到的问题。 对于我们其他人来说，拥有领域知识可以跳过很多来回，因为您通常已经拥有做出决策所需的知识。
  • 在确定新项目或功能的范围时，领域知识非常有用。 拥有领域知识（让您知道要构建什么）和技术经验（了解如何将其融入现有产品）是弄清楚 MVP 是什么样子时需要具备的良好技能。
  • 这很有趣。 在我看来，了解一个新的行业和领域是构建软件最酷的事情之一。

知识类型

在我看来，您可以将有效构建软件所需的知识分为四类（非常粗略）：

基础知识

有关计算机及其构建模块如何工作的基础知识。 这可能与处理器如何工作、算法的复杂性、客户端/服务器基础设施、何时以及如何缓存等有关。

这种知识通常是通过正规教育获得的，但也可以通过阅读书籍获得，有时也可以通过工作中的惨痛教训获得。 这种知识通常适用于各种工作，并且在几乎所有情况下都很有用。

堆栈特定知识

然后是特定于您的堆栈的知识。 如何充分利用您的编程语言，它有哪些奇怪的怪癖或枪法2，或者如何最好地利用您的编程语言之上的框架。 如果您使用相同的堆栈，这些知识通常会在不同的工作中延续，但如果您从一个全新的堆栈开始，您将不得不重新学习新的堆栈特定知识。

产品知识

接下来是有关您目前正在开发的产品或服务的知识。 这通常总是针对具体工作的，这意味着当你换工作时，你总是必须重新学习这一点。 根据您公司拥有的产品数量，您甚至可能需要在同一份工作中多次学习这一点。

领域知识

最后是有关最终用户所在领域的知识。 我个人认为领域知识非常广泛，很难定义，但这里有一些例子：

  • 当使用风力涡轮机软件时，即使我不需要与风力涡轮机进行物理交互，了解很多关于风力涡轮机如何工作的信息也非常方便。 我阅读了《风能手册》，实际上最终向业内其他公司教授了“软件开发人员风力涡轮机”课程。
  • 在 Climatiq 进行碳排放估算时，阅读相关标准（例如关于碳核算如何运作的《温室气体协议企业标准》），帮助我比每次都需要有人向我解释相关背景更快地做出许多决策。
  • 当我在废物管理行业创业时，我们采访了废物运输商，实际上还与其中一个人一起工作了一整天，看看他们的日常生活是什么样的。

正如您所看到的，领域知识可以采取多种形式。 上面的三个例子涵盖了物理/机械约束、行业标准和最终用户行为/心态。

最后，我们试图获得足够的全局知识，以便我们可以轻松地设身处地为用户着想，从而做出考虑到他们的正确决策。

如何学习领域知识

我认为我们获取不同类型知识的方式不同。

我认为可以公平地说，大部分产品知识和堆栈知识都来自日常工作。 当开始新的堆栈或产品时，您可能会做一些教程并阅读一些指南，但之后学习自然会成为常规工作流程的一部分。

另一方面，我认为基础知识和领域知识都需要明确的“学习时间”。

这是因为他们经常需要从摆在我们面前的任务中退一步来着眼于更大的前景。

虽然基础知识通常是您在教育中学到的东西，但领域知识却不是，因为它也是如此。 特定领域的。

获取领域知识的一些方法是：

  • 阅读您所在领域的入门教科书
  • 参加您所在领域的入门课程
  • 与使用您产品的最终用户交谈。

我想我们中的许多人可能会觉得我们太忙了，无法退后一步并安排时间来获取我们所需的领域知识。 但我们应该花点时间。 从长远来看，这是有回报的。

  1. 根据您使用的系统有多大，您可能有软件模块的用户，并且可能有最终用户。 在足够大的系统中，这种区别可能会变得相当模糊，但让我们考虑了解最终用户以及任何直接用户，作为相关领域知识的一部分。 ↩
  2. Python 可变默认参数，我在看着你。 ↩
